serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Paneer 65 is approximately 200 g, consisting of several pieces of deep-fried spiced paneer served as a starter or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer (Indian cottage cheese), fried — nutrient composition estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) paneer raw values adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN (2017) reports raw paneer at approximately 265 kcal, 18.3 g protein, 20.8 g fat, 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Deep-frying with gram flour batter and oil absorption increases energy, fat, and carbohydrate. Adapted values reflect a typical Paneer 65 preparation: batter coating adds ~8–10 g carbohydrate per 100 g final product; oil absorption adds ~5–7 g fat per 100 g final product. Final per-100 g values are estimated composites consistent with published analyses of similar South Asian fried paneer dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of paneer-based and fried Indian snack foods — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried cheese-based snacks and gram flour (besan) preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Paneer 65 exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 48 based on: (1) paneer (fresh cheese) has a very low GI (~15–27) due to high protein and fat content; (2) gram flour (besan) batter has a GI of approximately 39–44 (University of Sydney GI Database, besan/chickpea flour entries); (3) deep-frying slows gastric emptying, further lowering effective GI. Composite estimate of GI 48 is conservative and defensible. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (48 × 11.0 × 2.0) / 100 = 10.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Paneer 65 to small portions due to its moderate sodium content (~840 mg per restaurant serving) and high saturated fat, both of which can elevate blood pressure; request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from gram flour batter and high fat content affecting insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer 65 has a low glycemic index (~48) due to its high protein and fat content, but the gram flour batter contributes approximately 22 g of carbohydrate per restaurant serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in moderation and account for the carbohydrate load in their meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.5 g per 100 g), cholesterol (~52 mg per 100 g), and deep-frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Paneer 65 is high in saturated fat and total fat from both the paneer and deep-frying process; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food and prefer baked or air-fried paneer preparations to reduce saturated fat and total caloric intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer 65 is a popular North Indian deep-fried appetiser made from paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) that has been marinated in a spiced yoghurt and gram flour (besan) batter, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is characterised by its bold, fiery flavour profile featuring red chilli, ginger-garlic paste, curry leaves, and aromatic spices such as cumin, coriander, and garam masala. The exterior is crisp and lightly battered while the interior remains soft and creamy, reflecting the characteristic texture of paneer. The name '65' is commonly associated with the style of preparation popularised in South and North Indian restaurant cuisine, analogous to Chicken 65. Paneer 65 is widely served as a starter, snack, or side dish in Indian restaurants across Singapore and globally. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~320 kcal per 100 g) with significant protein (~14.5 g) from paneer and high fat (~24 g) from both the cheese and frying oil, including notable saturated fat (~9.5 g). The gram flour batter contributes moderate carbohydrate (~11 g per 100 g). It is a good source of calcium (~280 mg per 100 g) and vitamin B12 (~0.5 mcg per 100 g) from the dairy component. Clinically, it is a high-calorie, high-fat dish that should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing cardiovascular disease, hypertension, or Type 2 Diabetes. It is suitable for lacto-vegetarians and is generally considered halal.
common_user_queries: What is the difference between Paneer 65 and Chilli Paneer?
What spices are used in Paneer 65?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Paneer 65 exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN raw paneer data with defensible adjustments for deep-frying and gram flour batter. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for besan and paneer. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is well-grounded and the ingredient composition is well-established.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) raw paneer baseline with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption and gram flour batter coating; no exact prepared-dish entry exists in approved databases. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for paneer or fried paneer preparations. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for paneer. 4. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Paneer 65 exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The input food_name 'Panner 65' is a common phonetic misspelling; canonical English name corrected to 'Paneer 65'. 6. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish requirements (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in a Singapore restaurant context. 7. Gram flour (besan) may contain trace gluten depending on processing facility; is_gluten_free set to false as a precaution. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100 g × serving size in 100 g units) / 100 = (48 × 11.0 × 2.0) / 100 = 10.56, rounded to 10.6.
